results.possibleIdentity: Bernard Childs (1910–1985) was an influential American painter, printmaker, and sculptor known for his innovative use of technology and industrial tools in art. Born in Brooklyn and active in both New York and Paris, he is best recognized for pioneering a technique called "power drypoint," which involved using high-speed industrial power tools—such as drills and rotary burrs—to engrave metal plates directly. His work was highly experimental, often bridging the gap between abstraction and figuration. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, Childs expanded his artistic practice into three-dimensional illuminated sculptures made from engraved acrylic sheets (Plexiglas) that were lit from below to explore light, color, and symbolic motifs. This interest in combining art with technological and industrial elements aligns with the spirit of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization active during that period. His work was featured in a major retrospective at the Storm King Art Center in 1969, which showcased his paintings, prints, and light sculptures. Throughout his career, he maintained studios at the Hotel Chelsea in New York and in Paris, exhibiting extensively at venues like the Stedelijk Museum and the Tokyo International Print Biennial.

results.possibleIdentity: Larry A. Silverstein (born 1931) is primarily known as a billionaire real estate developer (Silverstein Properties) who rebuilt the World Trade Center. However, he has a strong historical connection to the arts, having graduated from the High School of Music and Art in New York City (Class of 1948). He and his wife, Klara, are prominent art collectors and patrons. Verifiable records from the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) associate his name with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), specifically as a supporter or donor listed in relation to the '9 evenings: theatre and engineering' event series and publications in 1966. Silverstein has described himself as being 'passionate about public space art' and has collaborated with numerous contemporary artists, including Frank Stella, Jeff Koons, and Jenny Holzer, to integrate art into his commercial real estate projects.

Lawrence (Larry) Richard Silverstein (1941–2025) was an artist and art educator active in New Jersey from the late 1960s through the early 2000s. He earned a degree in arts education from Jersey City State College in 1966 and spent 38 years teaching art in the Franklin Township school district. Alongside his teaching career, he was a member of the First Mountain Crafters artist guild and exhibited his work at various art shows. During the late 1960s and 1970s, he also worked as a movie theater projectionist at the Maplewood Movie Theater, a role he maintained for two decades while pursuing his creative practice.

Larry Silverstein was a physicist and researcher at the Polytechnic Institute of Brooklyn (Brooklyn Polytech) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He worked alongside laser inventor Gordon Gould and Donald Scarl on experimental physics projects involving electron beams and laser technology. Given the focus of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) on collaborations between artists and engineers/scientists, Silverstein's profile as a technical expert in New York during the peak E.A.T. years makes him a plausible candidate for a historical identity associated with the organization's technical or engineering membership.

results.possibleIdentity: Jane Holzer (born Jane Brukenfeld, October 23, 1940), widely known as "Baby Jane" Holzer, is an American art collector, film producer, and former actress and model who was a central figure in the New York art world of the 1960s and 1970s. She is best known as Andy Warhol’s first "Superstar," appearing in many of his early experimental films such as Soap Opera (1964), Couch (1964), Batman Dracula (1964), and Camp (1965). Holzer was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s as a model—featured on the cover of British Vogue in 1964—and as a socialite and fashion icon nicknamed the "Girl of the Year" by Tom Wolfe. She was closely associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; she and her husband, real estate developer Leonard Holzer, were members and supporters of the group. Leonard Holzer notably collaborated with E.A.T. to incorporate large-scale art installations by artists like Robert Rauschenberg and Alexander Calder into his property developments, such as the Smith Haven Mall in 1969. In her later career, Jane Holzer became a prolific art collector and a producer for films such as Kiss of the Spider Woman (1985) and Spring Breakers (2012).

results.possibleIdentity: Ron Cooper (Ronald Cooper, born 1943) is an American artist associated with the Light and Space and Finish Fetish movements of the 1960s and 1970s. He began his career in Los Angeles, where his work focused on light, reflection, and transparency using materials such as neon, fluorescent tubes, glass, and metal. In the mid-1960s, his studio in Los Angeles served as a venue for experimental workshops, including those involving Alex Hay, a performer central to the foundational '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' event organized by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Cooper is often mentioned in contexts involving the collaboration between artists and technology; his name appears in exhibition catalogs alongside other prominent tech-focused artists of the era like Robert Irwin and James Turrell. In 1969, he was identified as one of the artists in the 'dirty half dozen' featured in an Esquire article alongside figures like Yayoi Kusama, Carolee Schneemann, and Charlotte Moorman. In 1990, he transitioned into the culinary arts by founding the Del Maguey Single Village Mezcal brand, which he characterizes as 'liquid art.'

Robert Cooper (1931–2013) was a British social philosopher and organization theorist who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. While primarily known for his academic contributions at the Tavistock Institute of Human Relations, his work was deeply influenced by avant-garde poetry and systems theory. He edited the poetry magazine 'Artisan' in the 1950s and maintained connections with poets like Robert Creeley and Charles Olson. His influential 1976 paper, 'The Open Field,' drew upon these poetic roots to develop a process-oriented ontology of organization. Cooper's theoretical work often explored the intersection of technology and human organization (which he termed 'cyborganization'), focusing on the boundaries or 'seams' that both divide and connect entities. His work is recognized for its dense, poetic style that challenged the conventions of social science writing and aligned with the cross-disciplinary goals of organizations like E.A.T. that sought to link art, science, and technology.

results.possibleIdentity: Ingeborg Richter (1933–2020) was an artist who participated in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement during the late 1960s. She is specifically noted for her involvement in the 1968 exhibition "Some More Beginnings" at the Brooklyn Museum, which was organized by E.A.T. to showcase all entries from their competition for "The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age" show at MoMA. Richter's work during this period involved experimenting with new media and techniques, aligning with E.A.T.'s mission to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. Her records, alongside other E.A.T. participants, are preserved in the Experiments in Art and Technology Records at the Getty Research Institute.

Robert Richter is a prominent American documentary filmmaker and producer active in New York City since the 1960s. He founded Richter Productions and is known for creating sociopolitical and environmental documentaries. His 1970 film "The Gifts," which addressed water pollution, was nominated for an Academy Award. Richter was a significant figure in the New York independent film community during the late 1960s and 1970s, a period when the city's avant-garde and experimental film scenes frequently intersected with organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His career has been honored with retrospectives at institutions like the Firehouse Cinema (DCTV) in New York.

Hans Richter (1888–1976) was a pioneering German-born Dadaist artist and experimental filmmaker who was highly active in New York City from the 1940s through the early 1970s. As the Director of the Institute of Film Techniques at the City College of New York (1942–1956), he was a central figure in the development of the American avant-garde cinema. His work, such as "Rhythmus 21," is frequently cited in the context of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) as an early historical precursor to the fusion of art and technology. Although he was an older established artist by the late 1960s, he continued to create and exhibit new works, specifically focusing on complex collage techniques after setting aside painting in the late 1960s.

results.possibleIdentity: Mary Goldman Ascher (1900–1988) was a British-born American abstract painter, printmaker, and author active in New York City from the mid-20th century through the early 1980s. She studied at the New York School of Applied Design for Women, Hunter College, and the Art Students League under instructors such as Will Barnet and Morris Kantor. Ascher is particularly noted for her "Corridors of Power" series (1967), which explored institutional power dynamics, media, and the impact of technology on society; works in this series, such as "Automation II" and "Communication," reflect on the deindividuation of humans as they become increasingly reliant on machines. Her interest in the intersection of art, media, and technology led to her work being included in exhibitions such as "Visible Communication" at the Mishkin Gallery, alongside artists like Juan Downey. Beyond her thematic focus on technology, she was well-known for her religious and biblical cycles, including the portfolio "Twelve Women of the New Testament and Early Church" (c. 1964), which is held in the collection of the Vatican. Ascher served as the president of the American Society of Contemporary Artists from 1973 to 1975 and had a major 30-year retrospective at the National Arts Club in 1973.

results.possibleIdentity: Clifford Ross (born October 15, 1952) is an American multimedia artist, photographer, and filmmaker known for his large-scale works and significant technological innovations in the arts. He graduated from Yale University in 1974, where he was active in the early 1970s, notably curating the first comprehensive museum survey of Edward Gorey's work for his senior thesis. Ross began his professional career as a painter and sculptor in the mid-1970s, influenced by the Color Field movement and his aunt, Helen Frankenthaler. His work is characterized by a deep engagement with technology and collaboration, aligning with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; he is documented in the E.A.T. archives at the Whitney Museum. Ross is particularly famous for his 'Hurricane' series, for which he photographed extreme weather while tethered to an assistant on shore, and his 'Mountain' series of high-resolution landscape images. To achieve his artistic vision, he invented and patented specialized equipment, including the R1 high-resolution camera and the R2 360-degree video camera, developed alongside imaging scientists from Applied Minds, Inc. He also founded The Clifford Ross Company in 1985, which produced the animated series 'Babar'.

results.possibleIdentity: Maynard "Mike" Merle Whitney (1931–2012) was an American sculptor and educator active during the 1960s and 1970s. Born in Cedar Rapids, Iowa, he earned a B.F.A. from the University of Nebraska and an M.F.A. from the University of Oregon (1965). He also studied at the College of Technology in Belfast, Northern Ireland. Whitney served as the Chair of the Art Department at Nebraska Wesleyan University from 1965 to 1977 and later held a similar position at Colorado Women's College. His artistic career progressed from early woodcarvings, such as "Red, White, and Black" (painted black walnut), to experimental works in the 1970s using primary-colored Plexiglas and geometric shapes, some of which were lighted (e.g., "Red Line #4"). His work is held in several collections, including the Joslyn Art Museum in Omaha, the Denver Art Museum, and the Sheldon Memorial Art Gallery. His use of light and industrial materials like Plexiglas in the late 1960s and 1970s aligns with the experimental and technological themes of organizations such as Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: Marilyn Wood (1929–2016) was an American dancer, choreographer, and intermedia artist who became a pioneer of site-specific performance art in the late 1960s and 1970s. She was a founding member of the Merce Cunningham Dance Company, performing with them from 1958 to 1963 and touring extensively with avant-garde luminaries such as John Cage and Robert Rauschenberg (a co-founder of Experiments in Art and Technology). In 1968, she founded 'Marilyn Wood and the Celebration Group,' a collective of dancers, visual artists, filmmakers, architects, and musicians focused on creating large-scale 'Celebrations' in urban environments. Her most celebrated project was 'Celebrations in City Places' at the Seagram Building in New York City (1972), which used the entire 44-story facade, lobby, and plaza for a performance featuring 35 dancers and interactive media. Her work frequently mirrored the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) by integrating technology, architecture, and engineering into public spectacle, and she was closely associated with the network of artists and engineers involved in the 9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering events. In 1987, she founded the International Center for Celebration (ICC) in Santa Fe, New Mexico, continuing her work as a 'choreographer of cities.'

results.possibleIdentity: J. Handel Evans is an architect, artist, and academic leader who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is specifically associated with the 'Experiments in Art and Technology' (E.A.T.) discourse through the journal Leonardo, where he appeared in a 1970 index under the E.A.T. heading. In 1966, he corresponded with Thomas Wilfred, a pioneer of kinetic art and 'Lumia' (art using electric light), regarding his thesis at the University of Oregon. Evans has a diverse background in architecture, having served as a professor of architecture and eventually the President of San Jose State University and the founding President of California State University Channel Islands (CSUCI). As an artist, he is known for creating large-scale abstract handwoven tapestries, which have been collected by institutions such as the Mingei Museum and the Art Institute of Chicago. He was also instrumental in establishing Studio Channel Islands, an art center on the CSUCI campus.

John Evans (1932–2012) was a prominent New York-based collage and mail artist who was highly active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Beginning in 1964, Evans famously committed to creating a small collage every single day in bound notebooks, a project he continued until the year 2000. These daily collages often incorporated 'flotsam and jetsam'—found objects and ephemera from the streets of the East Village, where he lived and worked. His work is closely associated with the New York avant-garde and mail art movements of the era. While primarily known for his daily practice, his association with the broader experimental art scene in New York during the peak of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement makes him a relevant historical identity. His archives and works are held in collections such as the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) through the Political Art Documentation/Distribution (PAD/D) archive.

results.possibleIdentity: Donald Little is a musician and educator who was active as a performing artist beginning in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is most notable for his work as a tuba artist and his involvement with the experimental new music ensemble BL Lacerta. The ensemble was known for its avant-garde performances and collaborated with high-profile experimental artists such as John Cage, Pauline Oliveros, and Stuart Dempster. Little received his Bachelor of Music in 1970 from the Peabody Conservatory and his Master of Music in 1971 from Northwestern University. In 1976, he joined the faculty of the University of North Texas College of Music. His career involves a intersection of music and experimental performance, which aligns with the spirit of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) that promoted cross-disciplinary collaborations between artists and technical specialists.

Don Little was a technical specialist who served as the head of the instrumentation and photography section at the Suffield Experimental Station in Alberta, Canada, during the 1960s. In this role, he managed a team responsible for high-speed photography and complex instrumentation for scientific experiments, often collaborating with researchers from organizations like Bell Telephone (Bell Labs). Given that E.A.T. (Experiments in Art and Technology) was founded by Bell Labs engineers and specifically sought to connect artists with technical experts in fields like high-speed photography and electronics, this Don Little represents the type of technical collaborator frequently associated with the movement during the late 1960s.

The name 'Don Little' may also be a reconciliation of the 'Don Banks Music Box' (DBMB), which was frequently referred to by its developers and users as 'Don's little grey box.' The device was one of the first portable electronic music synthesizers, developed in the late 1960s (circa 1967-1968) for the Australian composer and artist Don Banks (1923–1980) by the Electronic Music Studios (EMS) team in London. Don Banks was an artist active in the late 1960s and 1970s whose work focused on the intersection of traditional composition and new electronic technology, a mission very similar to that of E.A.T.

results.possibleIdentity: Joan Webster Price (born 1931 in Camden, New Jersey) is an American abstract artist, sculptor, and educator known for her work in printmaking and her innovative "solar energy sculptures." She was active from the late 1960s through the late 1990s and beyond, often collaborating with her husband, Herbert Price. Her artistic career is defined by her involvement in the American Abstract Artists (AAA) group and her interest in the intersection of art and technology. She and Herbert Price created stainless-steel sculptures that integrated solar panels or thermal hot water systems, such as the "Solar Arch" (1997), intended to serve as both aesthetic monuments and functional energy-capturing devices. Price was a long-time Professor in the Art Department at the City College of New York (CCNY). Her work is represented in major collections, including the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Smithsonian American Art Museum, and the Brooklyn Museum. She is frequently listed as a member and supporter of the Leonardo/ISAST network and YLEM, organizations dedicated to artists working with science and technology, aligning with the ethos of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).
